text: The laparoscopic camera enters through the umbilical port, revealing the right iliac fossa with significant inflammation. The base of the appendix appears intact, while the tip and body are visibly inflamed and adhered to the small intestine, forming a lump with phlegmon.

The surgeon uses a traumatic grasper to separate the edematous appendix from the small intestine. The appendix appears swollen and thrombosed, resembling a fecolith, with visible risk of perforation if not addressed promptly.

The surgeon uses a semi-traumatic grasper to lift the appendix, revealing adhesions and inflammation at its base. An anterometrial traction is applied, and the harmonic scalpel is used to separate the appendix from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to skeletonize the base of the appendix, visible near the tinea coli and phlegmons, with the ileocecal junction located below.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect the mesoappendix near the tinea coli, ensuring the teflon jaw is oriented towards the appendix to avoid damage. The left hand provides anteromedial traction while the right hand completes the separation, reaching the base of the appendix.

The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix, which appears clear and unaffected by inflammation. Using a knot pusher, an extracorporeal misrash knot is applied to ligate the base near the C curve, forming a secure dumbbell-shaped knot.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut the appendix, leaving a five-millimeter stump at the base. The harmonic scalpel is then used to burn the remaining mucosa, ensuring disinfection and preventing mucosal remnants. Minimal fluid and slight bleeding are observed, which are suctioned out from the fossa. Betadine is applied to the area, and the appendix is removed.

The surgeon injects 5 ml of betadine into the surgical site to address any remaining inflammation or infection, then completely suctions it out. A 10 mm telescope is replaced with a 5 mm telescope introduced into the left iliac fossa. An endo catch bag with a metal ring and self-retaining feature is used to secure the appendix.
The surgeon uses a 5mm telescope to visualize the appendix being extracted through the umbilical port using Oham forceps. The mouth of the appendix is opened outside the body for removal.
